issues.criterion: feature_list_005.json feature 038 acceptance core / contract design_notes.nature_of_this_feature and design_notes.schema_design: 'Exceptions require structured approval state' -- the contract's own prose commits to an 'operator_approval' object whose three sub-fields (approver, approved_at, authorization) are each required to be a non-empty string whenever verdict is PASS_WITH_EXCEPTIONS, per ecosystem/01_clean_state_preflight.md Sec 5's Operator-Exception Rule ('the operator's decision -- identity, timestamp, and the specific exceptions accepted -- is recorded in the same verdict artifact').
issues.evidence: Built two SCRATCH prototype schemas (never touching any real repo path) implementing the two textually-plausible readings of design_notes.schema_design's own words: Variant A ('operator_approval': type object, additionalProperties true, with a 'properties' block typing approver/approved_at/authorization as non-empty strings but WITHOUT an internal 'required' array of its own -- i.e. the sub-fields are typed IF present but none of them is actually mandatory) and Variant B (identical, but operator_approval also carries its own 'required': ['approver','approved_at','authorization']). Both variants pass jsonschema.Draft202012Validator.check_schema and satisfy every one of contract 038's OWN literal, unmodified verification commands identically: ran entries 3-9 (extracted programmatically from the live .agent/contracts/038.json, not retyped) from two disposable scratch roots, each containing ONLY the respective schema at schema/preflight_verdict.schema.json and the five fixtures exactly as contract 038's own files_create purposes describe them (bare PASS; bare FAIL; PASS_WITH_EXCEPTIONS with a complete operator_approval; an unsupported-verdict negative; and a PASS_WITH_EXCEPTIONS-with-exceptions-but-NO-operator_approval-key negative) -- entries 3,4,5,6,7,8,9 all print PASS under BOTH variants, with byte-identical output. A sixth, additional GAP-PROBE instance -- {'verdict':'PASS_WITH_EXCEPTIONS','exceptions':[{...}],'operator_approval':{}} (the key present, but recording literally zero approver identity, timestamp, or authorization) -- VALIDATES (is ACCEPTED) under Variant A and is REJECTED ('approver' is a required property) under Variant B. Because none of the contract's 24 verification entries construct or test this instance (entry 5 only tests operator_approval's total ABSENCE; the two named negative fixtures per the contract's own files_create purposes only cover 'unsupported verdict value' and 'no operator_approval key at all', never 'operator_approval present but empty/incomplete'; entry 7's positive-fixture check only proves a COMPLETE operator_approval validates, never that an incomplete one is rejected), the entire 24-entry suite is provably unable to distinguish an implementation that genuinely enforces 'structured' approval (identity + timestamp + authorization, each non-empty, per the contract's own design_notes.schema_design prose) from one that accepts a vacuous {} in that slot -- which would satisfy the PASS_WITH_EXCEPTIONS conditional's letter while recording none of the substance Sec 5's Operator-Exception Rule requires. This is not a hypothetical: it is reproduced live, twice, with the contract's own literal command text, unmodified.
issues.required_fix: Add at least one new verification entry (or extend an existing fixture set with a third named negative fixture, e.g. tools/fixtures/preflight_verdict_invalid_approval_incomplete.json) that instantiates PASS_WITH_EXCEPTIONS with a non-empty exceptions array AND an operator_approval object that is present but incomplete -- at minimum: (a) operator_approval: {} (all three sub-fields absent), and ideally also (b)-(d) each of the three sub-fields independently missing, and (e) each independently present as an empty string -- and assert jsonschema.ValidationError is raised in every one of those cases, mirroring the rigor already applied to the three-verdict enum (entries 4-6). This closes the gap between the contract's own textual commitment ('each a non-empty string') and what its verification suite actually proves, and forces the real schema/preflight_verdict.schema.json to declare operator_approval's own 'required' array (or an equivalent minLength/if-then enforcement) rather than leaving that decision unverified and up to the generator's own, unchecked, interpretation.

missing_acceptance_coverage: feature_list_005.json feature 038 acceptance criterion 'Exceptions require structured approval state' is only partially covered: the 24-entry suite proves operator_approval's KEY must be present when verdict is PASS_WITH_EXCEPTIONS (entry 5, and the 'invalid_exceptions' named negative fixture in entry 9), but no entry proves the key's CONTENT must be structurally complete (approver/approved_at/authorization each present and non-empty), which is the actual substance of 'structured approval state' per ecosystem/01_clean_state_preflight.md Sec 5 and per the contract's own design_notes.schema_design prose.
unsupported_claims: design_notes.schema_design's statement that operator_approval is '(object: approver/approved_at/authorization, each a non-empty string)' is a specific structural claim about the schema this contract proposes to build, but it is not backed by any verification entry: none of the 24 commands constructs an instance with an incomplete or empty operator_approval object and asserts it is rejected. Demonstrated live (see issues[0].evidence) that two schemas differing exactly on this point -- one enforcing the claimed structure, one not -- both pass the entire verification suite identically.
observations: SCHEMA VALIDITY: `python3 -c "import json,jsonschema; jsonschema.validate(json.load(open('.agent/contracts/038.json')), json.load(open('schema/contract.schema.json')))"` re-run as part of this review: exits 0, no error. Contract 038 (revision 0) is schema-valid against schema/contract.schema.json.

ARRAY LENGTH: parsed .agent/contracts/038.json's 'verification' array programmatically: exactly 24 entries, matching evidence_convention's own stated count ('the verification array has exactly 24 entries') and the .agent/evidence/038-verify-01.txt .. -24.txt naming scheme it describes. No off-by-one.

FULL 24-ENTRY BASELINE RE-RUN (all 24 commands extracted programmatically from the live 038.json, not retyped, executed from the repository root against the live pre-implementation tree, tools/validate.sh confirmed independently to report 'SUMMARY: 11 passed, 0 failed' at baseline with contracts 034.json/038.json and v0.6.0-release-notes.md already present as untracked files): entries 1, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 12, 13, 15 (all content-anchored to the not-yet-created schema/NIZAM.json-edit/README.md-edit) correctly FAIL pre-implementation (schema/preflight_verdict.schema.json is absent from both the working tree and HEAD, confirmed independently by `git show HEAD:schema/preflight_verdict.schema.json` failing; NIZAM.json and schema/README.md are unmodified). Entries 2, 11, 14, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 22, 23, 24 all currently PASS on the clean pre-implementation tree, exactly as the task's own precedent-anchored expectation predicted -- entries 2/11/14 are the anti-vacuity master proofs (each correctly confirms the corresponding content-anchored target is absent/unindexed today, so the paired content check is non-vacuous by construction); entries 16-24 are regression/non-goal/scope/immutability guards that are meant to hold both before and after a compliant implementation, not to discriminate this feature's own implementation state (matching the treatment the 033-contract-review precedent gave its own equivalent entry 9 -- 'a pure regression check... not meant to discriminate this feature's own implementation state').

SECTION-ANCHORED CHECK (entries 13/14, using tools/verify_lib.sh's vlib_section_grep) PROVEN GENUINELY DISCRIMINATING, not vacuous to a whole-document grep: built a GOOD scratch copy of the real schema/README.md with a new Schemas-table row naming preflight_verdict.schema.json (vlib_section_grep -> PASS) and a BAD scratch copy with the identical row removed but the literal string 'preflight_verdict.schema.json' instead planted in the unrelated '## DD-3' section (vlib_section_grep -> correctly FAILS, exit 1) -- while a plain whole-file `grep -q` against the same BAD fixture matches (would have been a false pass). Both fixtures are scratch-only copies; the real schema/README.md was read but never written to.

GLOB-ORDER INDEPENDENCE (entry 9, direct probe of the task's own concern): entry 9's python source (re-read from the live contract, not retyped) hardcodes the two negative fixture filenames as a literal list -- `['tools/fixtures/preflight_verdict_invalid_verdict.json', 'tools/fixtures/preflight_verdict_invalid_exceptions.json']` -- with no glob.glob call anywhere in its body. Its correctness therefore cannot depend on filesystem glob ordering at all, unlike entry 8 alone (which does use glob.glob('tools/fixtures/*preflight*') and picks neg[0]). Confirmed no other file under the live tools/fixtures/ directory contains the substring 'preflight' today, so entry 8's glob is not currently at risk of accidental cross-contamination either; entry 9 independently closes the ordering concern regardless. This is the correct, house-precedent-consistent fix for the glob-order defect class (contrast contract 037's still-glob-only F-008/AT literal in feature_list_005.json, which this contract's entry 9 deliberately supplements).

MISSPELLED-VERDICT-KEY / ADDITIONALPROPERTIES PROBE (task's specific concern): tested an instance carrying both a wrong-case 'Verdict' key AND the correct lowercase 'verdict' key -- validates (harmless, the correct required key is present and enum-checked normally; additionalProperties:true merely tolerates the stray extra key). Tested an instance carrying ONLY the wrong-case 'Verdict' key (no lowercase 'verdict' at all) -- correctly REJECTED ('verdict' is a required property), under both scratch schema variants. additionalProperties does not create a bypass for the required top-level 'verdict' key or its enum constraint; this concern is probed and found to be a non-issue.

SCOPE-GUARD / BYTE-IDENTITY GUARD DESIGN (entries 20, 24): entry 20 (`git diff --quiet HEAD -- schema/ ':(exclude)schema/README.md'`) alone would NOT detect a hypothetical unauthorized NEW (untracked) schema/*.json file, since `git diff HEAD` is blind to untracked paths. This is not a live-probed finding (the ABSOLUTE PROBE-ISOLATION RULE forbids creating any file under the real schema/ tree, even a reverted one) but is confirmed analytically from tools/verify_lib.sh's own vlib_scope_guard source (already read in full): entry 24 invokes vlib_scope_guard, which runs `git status --porcelain --untracked-files=all -- . ':(exclude).agent'` and would flag any untracked schema/*.json path not in its allow-list (only 'schema/preflight_verdict.schema.json' and 'schema/README.md' are allow-listed for schema/). Entries 20 and 24 are therefore complementary, not redundant: 20 catches modification of pre-existing tracked schema files, 24 catches unauthorized new untracked ones. No gap found in this pairing.

CONCURRENCY: read the live .agent/contracts/034.json. Confirmed structurally consistent with 038's concurrency_fence claims: 034's own files_create is exactly ['ecosystem/02_evidence_baseline.md'] and files_modify is exactly ['ecosystem/README.md'], matching what 038's design_notes.concurrency_fence and non_goals describe as the two paths 038 must never touch or assert about. No edit was made to any 034 surface, .agent/contracts/034.json, or any .agent/qa/034*.json during this review.

REPOSITORY LEFT UNMODIFIED: `git status --porcelain --untracked-files=all` before and after this entire review shows the identical 3-path set (`.agent/contracts/034.json`, `.agent/contracts/038.json`, `v0.6.0-release-notes.md`, all untracked, plus this review's own new `.agent/qa/038-contract-review.json`) and `git diff HEAD --stat` is empty. All scratch schema variants, fixture roots, and README probe copies live only under the session scratchpad directory, outside the repository tree; nothing under schema/, ecosystem/, tools/, docs/, or NIZAM.json was created, edited, or deleted at any point in this review. No edit was made to .agent/run_state.json.

MINOR, NON-BLOCKING: entry 16's change_log/version-match check re-runs successfully at HEAD today (pre-implementation) because the pre-existing 0.4.0 version already has its own matching 0.4.0 change_log entry from a prior feature -- this is expected, self-consistency-only behavior (it does not compare against HEAD the way entry 15 does), not a vacuity defect: post-implementation, if the generator bumps the version to 0.5.0 without adding a matching 0.5.0 change_log entry, entry 16 would correctly fail. Also noted, non-blocking: entry 16's assertion only checks that a change_log entry's version key matches the bumped version, not that the entry's summary text actually describes the schema addition -- consistent with how every other schema/README.md change_log entry in this house is verified (or rather, not verified) for prose content elsewhere in the framework; not a defect specific to this contract.

final_verdict.summary: REJECTED at revision 0. Twenty-three of the twenty-four verification commands are sound, non-vacuous, and load-bearing, independently re-run (not trusted from the contract's own claims) using the literal, programmatically-extracted command strings against the live pre-implementation working tree: content-anchored entries (1, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 12, 13, 15) correctly FAIL today (schema/preflight_verdict.schema.json is absent from both the working tree and HEAD, NIZAM.json and schema/README.md are unmodified); the anti-vacuity master proofs (2, 11, 14) and the regression/non-goal/scope/immutability guards (16-24) all currently PASS, exactly matching this review's own live re-execution and the task's precedent-anchored expectation. The section-anchored README check (13/14) was proven genuinely discriminating against a cross-section-leakage scratch fixture (not a whole-file grep in disguise); the two named negative fixtures (entry 9) were confirmed independent of filesystem glob ordering by direct source inspection (no glob call in entry 9's body); the additionalProperties posture was probed and found not to create a misspelled-verdict-key bypass; the entry-20/entry-24 scope-guard pairing was confirmed complementary via tools/verify_lib.sh's own vlib_scope_guard source. However, a genuine, reproduced HIGH-severity gap was found in the schema's central 'structured operator approval' requirement: two disposable scratch schema variants -- one that internally requires operator_approval's three sub-fields (approver, approved_at, authorization) whenever present, and one that types those sub-fields but never makes them mandatory -- were each dropped into a scratch root alongside fixture sets matching the contract's own described fixture content, and every one of the contract's 24 verification commands (entries 3-9, the only entries that touch the schema's content at all) passed IDENTICALLY under both variants. A sixth probe instance -- PASS_WITH_EXCEPTIONS with a non-empty exceptions array and operator_approval: {} (present, but recording zero identity/timestamp/authorization) -- validates under the loose variant and is rejected only under the strict one, proving the contract's own textual commitment ('each a non-empty string', design_notes.schema_design) that operator_approval must be structurally complete is an unsupported claim: nothing in the verification suite would catch a generator that implements the loose reading, silently defeating ecosystem/01_clean_state_preflight.md Sec 5's Operator-Exception Rule (identity, timestamp, and the accepted exceptions must actually be recorded) from feature 038 onward, with downstream consumers (041's CLI, 043's dogfood run) inheriting a schema that only checks for the KEY's presence, not its substance. Required before re-review: add verification coverage (a new fixture and/or a direct inline check, mirroring entries 4-6's rigor for the verdict enum) that constructs a PASS_WITH_EXCEPTIONS instance with an incomplete or empty operator_approval object and proves it is rejected, and correspondingly commit the real schema to declaring operator_approval's own required sub-fields rather than leaving that decision unverified. Not approved for implementation as-is; approve only after this gap is closed and the fix is proven non-vacuous with the same loose-vs-strict variant comparison used in this review.
